nativeRange.habitat: Slow-moving and still waters in creeks, lakes, and swamps with dense aquatic or overhanging surface vegetation

diet.notes: The African Butterflyfish is primarily insectivorous and feeds exclusively at the water surface. It generally takes live and frozen foods such as bloodworm, brine shrimp, prawn, and small mealworms. Some individuals gradually learn to accept floating dried foods, but live and frozen prey tends to be strongly preferred. Any small fish or invertebrate that fits in its mouth is also at risk of being taken as food.

avoidWith: small fish under 3 cm (will be eaten)
fin-nipping species (long trailing fins may be nipped)

imageCandidates.notes: Photo by Toniher; 1001x685. Iconic sharp lateral view showing the enlarged wing-like pectoral fins spread and dead-leaf body in a planted tank; clearest diagnostic shot.
Photo by Vassil (Cologne Zoo); 1666x809. Three individuals at the surface, front view with pectoral 'wings' spread; shows natural surface-dwelling behavior and multiple specimens.
Photo by Isidro Martinez; 595x500. Striking top-down view showing both pectoral fins fully fanned like butterfly wings; unique angle, smaller resolution so kept as extra.

summary: The African Butterflyfish is a surface-dwelling predator from the slow, still blackwater pools of Central and West Africa, recognizable by its enlarged, wing-like pectoral fins. It feeds on insects and small prey at the water surface and is an extreme escape risk — a gap-free lid is essential.
careNotes:
Pantodon buchholzi is the sole species in both its genus and its family (Pantodontidae), making it unique among aquarium fish. It is widely regarded as a living fossil with origins traced to the Cretaceous period, and it is the closest living relative of the arowanas (family Osteoglossidae).

The African Butterflyfish is a crepuscular, largely sedentary ambush predator that resembles a dead leaf floating at the surface — its enlarged, wing-like pectoral fins provide camouflage and enable powerful jumps of several metres. ESCAPE RISK IS EXTREME: the species is physiologically built to glide and jump clear of the water, and even the smallest gap in a lid can prove fatal. A tightly sealed, gap-free lid is non-negotiable. The tank should be dimly lit with abundant floating plants (such as Indian fern or Ceratopteris) to mimic its natural surface habitat. A dark substrate tends to reduce skittishness. Minimal water current is preferred, and soft, slightly acidic, tannin-stained water closely matches the blackwater conditions of its native biotope.

NEVER keep with small fish: any tankmate small enough to fit in this fish's mouth is at risk of being eaten. Neon tetras, small rasboras, guppies, and similarly sized species are effectively prey items. All tankmates must be large and robust enough not to be consumed.

Feeding can be challenging. The African Butterflyfish is a specialist insectivore that tends to prefer live or frozen prey such as crickets, mealworms, fruit flies, and frozen mysis shrimp. Some individuals learn to accept floating prepared foods over time, but many refuse, and persistent refusal creates a real starvation risk. Live feeding is frequently necessary, at least during the acclimation period.

The species is typically kept singly or in male-female pairs due to conspecific aggression; larger groups may work in very spacious, heavily planted aquariums. Compatible tankmates must be too large to be eaten and should not compete for the surface layer. A tank with a long footprint and generous surface area is more important than water depth.
breedingNotes: The African Butterflyfish is an egg-scatterer and is considered difficult to breed in captivity. A dedicated breeding aquarium of at least 120 x 38 cm with a generous cover of floating plants — Indian fern is commonly recommended — is needed. Conditioning the pair with live or frozen insects over several weeks helps bring them into breeding condition. Spawning can often be triggered by temporarily lowering the water level, then refilling to a depth of 15–20 cm with slightly warmer, very soft, acidic water (pH 6.0–6.5, 25–27°C). Females may deposit over 100 eggs per day among the floating plants. The eggs typically hatch within 3–4 days. Fry are extremely small and can be difficult to raise; newly hatched brine shrimp are generally required as a first food. Adults show no parental care and may consume both eggs and fry if not separated.
